key_insight: Raising argon flow from 1600 to 1800 L/h during air plasma spraying produced denser NiCrFeBSiC coatings with thicker deposits, lower porosity, and better wear behavior.
engineering_relevance: Process gas tuning is a practical lever for improving thermal-sprayed self-fluxing coatings on the same substrate without changing alloy chemistry.
method: Air plasma sprayed NiCrFeBSiC self-fluxing metal-ceramic coatings at two argon flow rates: 1600 and 1800 L/h.
Compared coating structure and properties using thickness/porosity analysis, microhardness and elastic modulus measurements, tribology tests, and scratch adhesion testing.
Evaluated wear performance with ball-on-disk friction testing and interfacial strength with critical load measurements.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports about a twofold increase in coating thickness, a twofold decrease in porosity, microhardness of 1015.8 ± 81.5 HV, elastic modulus of 203.9 ± 5.4 GPa, and a lower friction coefficient near 0.107 at 1800 L/h.
watch_item: The study compares only two argon flow settings, so broader operating windows and long-term durability still need validation before process transfer.
paper_title: EFFECT OF ARGON FLOW RATE DURING AIR PLASMA SPRAYING ON THE STRUCTURE AND PROPERTIES OF NICRFEBSIC COATINGS
